data.title: Silvercorp Metals: Buy On Operational Success And Silver Demand
data.description:
Silvercorp Metals (SVM) is a Canada-based precious metals producer focused on the acquisition, exploration, development, and mining of silver-rich polymetallic deposits, with core operations in China’s Ying Mining District and GC Mine and growth projects in Ecuador that add gold and copper exposure in the future. Operating within the precious and base metals mining industry, SVM generates revenue primarily from silver, gold, lead, and zinc production, positioning it to benefit from both industrial demand and safe-haven investment flows. SVM offers leveraged exposure to precious metal prices with potential upside from operational efficiency, exploration success, and development-stage project execution, while remaining sensitive to commodity price volatility, geopolitical factors, and capital allocation discipline typical of cyclical resource producers.

I rate SVM a buy. FY26 Q3 earnings were solid, and the price of silver should remain high on strong demand. Production has been flat, but operations are becoming exceptionally efficient, which has led to great revenue and cash growth. Bottom-line numbers are to be digested, but suggest more growth is on the way soon.

Earnings Thoughts

SVM reported a huge earnings beat for Q3 FY26 last week with an adjusted EPS of $0.22, beating by $0.14, and more than doubling their result from last year's Q3. Revenue was also up big, over 50% higher YoY, to a record $126.1 million. The downside was the bottom-line result of a $15.8 million loss in net income, which I will go into further detail on later in the article.

Sales at their Ying Ming District, which is currently the majority of the business, rose 35% through the first nine months of the FY from a year ago. Revenue is continually progressing for SVM, in large part due to the rapid rise in silver price, which is up 102% in the last 6 months.

Operating costs have remained relatively stable, so free cash flow has escalated quickly, at $89.6 million in Q3, up from $20.5 million a year ago.

Silver Demand and Outlook

Silver prices have climbed sharply over the past year due to a combination of structural supply deficits, booming industrial demand, and macroeconomic factors. Global mine output and recycling have struggled to keep pace with rising consumption, leaving the silver market in its fifth straight supply deficit, a chronic imbalance that tightens inventories and supports higher prices. Industrial demand has surged, especially from cleantech and high-growth sectors like solar energy, electric vehicles (EVs), advanced electronics, and data centers. Silver has served as the best non-precious metal conductor, meaning it will be embedded in global tech and clean energy trends for the foreseeable future. Jewelry and silverware demand has declined, but the rise in the above-mentioned areas is heavily outpacing older silver industry uses.

The silver price is much higher than it’s ever been. SVM is reliant on this for revenue and earnings growth, which leaves me a little worried. However, I think that the silver price may remain elevated, at least for the near- to mid-term future, and on top of that, SVM is hedging their silver bet with upcoming mines in Ecuador that will specialize in other metals as well.

Silver Reliance and Risk

If you compare the SVM chart to that of silver futures, you will see they are awfully comparable. It’s no question that the stock has risen, from what feels almost entirely from the rise in silver. This is a risk to SVM, and as you can see above, silver in ounces sold actually fell from 2.0 million ounces to 1.9 million. Gold was up slightly in amount sold YoY but is such a minimal portion of business right now. I don’t suspect this is an issue moving forward, as demand for silver is heightening, but I think this metric will be critical to keep an eye on moving forward. Investors certainly should hope to see the number of ounces sold per quarter on the rise, especially with the uptick in demand.

Total production has remained fairly flat for SVM, but there are two pieces here that I think are positive. Their all-in sustaining cost (AISC) was also stable, rising just 1% YoY, while their total ore processed increased 15% on the year. This is showing that their throughput capacity and operational activity have been efficient. I imagine that this will lead to production gains in upcoming quarters, which should directly correlate to more metal sold.

Bottom-line Numbers

FY26 Q3 reported a net loss of $15.8 million. This can look like a scare to investors, but it must be noted that SVM saw a one-time $60.2 million non-cash charge on derivative liabilities related to convertible notes. The company has an adjusted net income of $47.9 million, up more than double from Q3 last year. They have seen 3 of these one-time charges in the past year from non-operating expenses, which has made for large fluctuations in net income.

Cash from operations soared to a record high of $132.9 million last quarter, up almost 3x YoY. Costs of mine operations were just 8% higher, and cash cost per ounce of silver was down. SVM has improved substantially from an operational perspective. Cash flow and sales are seeing the results, and net income will be in future quarters if they can manage non-operating expenses.

Conclusion

SVM is sitting in a good position as silver is near all-time highs. The demand for the metal is likely to stick around as it has become a need in several of the highest growth industries worldwide. Net loss was a disappointment for investors, but outside of a one-time non-operating expense, you can see that the company has made great strides in operations and is seeing the cash benefits. They will continue to utilize this cash toward expenditures in several mines across Ecuador that will enhance the firm’s ability to produce other metals outside of silver. Revenue from these mines isn’t expected until early 2027 but should really bolster their already growing Ying Ming silver mine. I like SVM as a buy.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Multi-Sector Bond ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The fourth quarter of 2025 maintained the trend of significant shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply-demand dynamics seen in the third quarter. Markets continued to rebound from post-“Liberation Day” lows as legal challenges, implementation delays, and immediate economic impacts remained muted. While tariffs and other policy outcomes are still pending, we should see some resolution from the Supreme Court in the coming months. However, financial markets remained confident in a solid economy, rangebound inflation, and early signs of easing geopolitical risks.

U.S. economic data continued to signal resilience despite a U.S. government shutdown that delayed or cancelled key releases. Once resolved, the post-shutdown data confirmed that certain trends remained intact: positive growth, contained inflation, and unemployment that held near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing, cutting rates by 25 basis points ('BPS') at both the October and December Federal Open Market Committee ('FOMC') meetings. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, Bank of England, and Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed: volatility re-emerged in the Russia/Ukraine conflict, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress. China’s military exercises late in the quarter around Taiwan were a reminder that tensions remain unresolved.

The ongoing market rebound drove broadly positive financial performance for 4Q. Fixed income sectors posted gains as front-end interest rates declined and most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries. The U.S. Treasury curve steepened, with the 2-year yield down by 13 bps, the 5-year yield down by 2 bps, the 10-year yield up by 2 bps, and the 30-year yield higher by 11 bps.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund returned 1.64% ('NAV') in the fourth quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index return of 1.10%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and delivered excess returns.
  • Emerging Market ('EM') Sovereigns: Allocation and selection within EM sovereigns were positive.
  • Investment Grade ('IG') Corporates: The Fund’s underweight and selection were positive.

Performance Detractors

  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities ('RMBS'): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') was negative.
  • High Yield ('HY') Corporate Securities Selection: Selection versus the benchmark was negative.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to cash, MBS, and IG corporates.
  • Reduced exposure to RMBS, asset-backed securities, and EM sovereigns.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund’s sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

EM Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan (JPM) EMBI Global Diversified Index ended 2025 on a strong note—its 3.29% return was supported by monetary easing, resilient technicals, and outperformance among select countries. HY credits led performance, while IG lagged amid modest spread volatility. Spreads tightened by 31 bps in 4Q to close the year at 253 bps. The J.P. Morgan CEMBI Global Diversified underperformed the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified, returning 1.29% amid significant volatility in Brazilian corporates during October. Local markets returned 3.34%, aided by a softer U.S. dollar and improving inflation. The Fund’s overall EM exposure was marginally lower in 4Q. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and EM HY over EM IG.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads widened 4 bps in 4Q to end 2025 in the high 70s, still at the very low end of the 20-year range. Yields were little changed at 4.81%. Supply was the big story as nearly $100 billion in issuance from large cloud providers set a new 4Q record. This caused some market indigestion even as demand drivers remained in place and flows stayed positive throughout the quarter. Credit metrics were stable while ratings drifted higher. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. With valuations tight, we favor a defensive posture, with a preference for U.S. banks, utilities, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index gained 1.31% in 4Q. Credit spreads were little changed, with the Index ending 1 bp tighter at 266 bps. The market continues to focus on inflation and its impact on rates, the outlook for artificial intelligence and related spending, and consumer spending. Finance companies, metals & mining, and gaming outperformed during the quarter. Cyclical industries such as packaging, retailers, and buildings products lagged as lower-quality names declined sharply into year-end.

Securitized: The securitized markets posted positive total and excess returns in 4Q, outperforming other credit sectors. Spreads tightened despite elevated issuance across all securitized sub-sectors as investors sought additional income and spread. Our exposure to agency MBS outperformed IG corporates as strong technicals, the Fed rate cut, and low rate volatility rewarded investors. While our securitized strategy continues to focus on up-in-quality credits such as agency MBS, we’re also selectively exploring transactions that present promising yield opportunities lower in the capital stack.

data.title: Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Performance Insights, Portfolio Drivers & Decisions
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors and Detractors to Quarter-End Fund Performance

Contributors

  • Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd. (TEVA)
  • Natera, Inc. (NTRA)
  • Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS)
  • Guardant Health, Inc. (GH)
  • Viking Holdings Ltd. (VIK)

Detractors

  • Commvault Systems, Inc. (CVLT)
  • Nintendo Co., Ltd. (NTDOY)
  • Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD)
  • Vistra Corp. (VST)
  • DoorDash, Inc. (DASH)
The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion of Contributors

The outlook is healthy for Teva Pharmaceutical Industries ('TEVA'). The recent quarter was a milestone for Teva Pharmaceuticals. Teva previously faced headwinds from declining generic sales and debt from past acquisitions. In early 2023, the company appointed a new CEO, marking a pivotal leadership change aimed at stabilizing operations, improving profitability, and reigniting growth. Under its “pivot to growth” strategy, Teva has expanded its biosimilars portfolio and strengthened its generics business, returning that segment to positive growth. However, the real engine of performance lies in Teva’s internally developed specialty medicines—AUSTEDO, AJOVY, and UZEDY—which are delivering robust revenue gains as adoption accelerates. In the latest quarter, Teva exceeded revenue expectations and achieved meaningful cost efficiencies, prompting S&P to upgrade its credit rating. With a growing pipeline of innovative treatments, disciplined financial management, and increasing market penetration, we believe that Teva is well positioned as a leading player in the global biopharmaceutical industry.

Natera, Inc. ('NTRA'), is transforming cancer care. A molecular diagnostics company, Natera specializes in three main areas of health care: women’s health, oncology, and organ health. The women’s health division has dominant market share in noninvasive prenatal testing market, generating wide margins that have fueled innovation across other segments. Leveraging in-house expertise, Natera developed Signatera, a liquid biopsy that detects minimal residual disease and can identify cancer recurrence months before traditional methods. The blood-based test currently holds FDA and Medicare approvals for a modest number of cancer types, yet adoption has surged as doctors embrace the minimally invasive approach and early detection capabilities. With additional approvals pending, Natera is investing heavily to drive awareness and volumes in 2026. Backed by cutting-edge technology and a vast underpenetrated market, Natera, we believe, is well-positioned to transform cancer care for years to come.

Carpenter Technology Corp. ('CRS') is prepared for lift-off. The company manufactures premium metal alloys used in highly specialized components across aerospace, medical, and industrial markets. While this diversification provides business stability, our investment thesis focuses on the significant replacement cycle in aerospace—a sector that has faced years of underinvestment and production delays. The post-pandemic surge in air travel has led airlines to keep aging fleets in service longer, driving higher maintenance needs. This has boosted demand for Carpenter’s suite of specialty bearings, fasteners, and electrical components.

Producing these advanced alloys is both complex and capital-intensive, giving Carpenter strong pricing power—a fact underscored this quarter as multiple long-term contracts were repriced favorably. With robust product demand and expanding margins, we remain confident in Carpenter’s long-term growth prospects.

Discussion of Detractors

AI concerns dented Commvault Systems, Inc. ('CVLT'). Commvault specializes in cyber security, housing complete backups of operating systems that provide companies with the opportunity to revert to prior versions in the event of cyber-attacks. Once known for legacy backup solutions, Commvault has successfully pivoted toward subscription-based and software-as-a-service offerings. However, the recent quarter produced mixed financial results with positive new business growth yet contracting profit margins.

Despite management signaling confidence in future execution, the rise of AI has caused intense scrutiny on potential disruption in the software industry. As a result, shares of Commvault sold off over the profitability concerns. Given heightened volatility and deteriorating visibility, we exited the position in favor of higher-conviction opportunities.

Robinhood Markets, Inc. ('HOOD'), hit pause but is still very much in the game. After a strong run through most of 2025, Robinhood saw a modest pullback in the fourth quarter as lower trading volumes in November tempered momentum. While net deposits and options-trading activity continued to rise, cryptocurrency transactions decelerated. Importantly, Robinhood is carving out a niche in prediction markets, processing more than half of Kalshi’s prediction options. Robinhood’s mobile-first approach and zero-minimum brokerage accounts have helped it become a top-five broker based on the number of funded accounts. Yet, despite this impressive penetration, Robinhood’s client base controls only about 1% of industry assets under management, leaving a long runway for growth. With innovation fueling engagement and demographic tailwinds, the recent dip looks more like a pause than a trend reversal in our view.

DoorDash, Inc. ('DASH'), hit a speed bump. DoorDash is a leader in the global digital delivery industry. The company has continued to leverage its scale and advanced logistics platform to expand beyond restaurant delivery into convenience, grocery, and retail categories. While core order volumes accelerated and top-line trends remained strong, the recent quarter was overshadowed by elevated spending. The company is aggressively investing in international expansion, technology upgrades, and new verticals. These investments pressured margins and tempered near-term profitability, sparking investor concerns despite healthy demand fundamentals. Longer term, we believe DoorDash’s diversification strategy—coupled with global market penetration and leadership in last-mile logistics—positions it to capture significant share in the evolving delivery ecosystem. We have maintained conviction in the position.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Platinum Price Tests $2,080 As Firmer Dollar Checks Rebound Dollar
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) held a mild upward bias Thursday after a choppy week, with spot prices hovering just above $2,070 as traders weighed a firmer dollar against broader precious metals support and an approaching U.S. inflation readout.

Price action: platinum follows the wider metals tape

Reuters reported at 3:36 AM UTC on February 19, 2026, that spot platinum rose 0.3% to $2,076.65 an ounce, extending a modest rebound as gold advanced and investors assessed U.S.-Iran tensions alongside fresh U.S. labor data.

The day’s platinum tone looked less like a breakout attempt and more like a “hold-the-gains” session, with price clustering near the mid-$2,070s rather than trending sharply. That matters after a month where intraday air pockets have repeatedly forced traders to treat round numbers as tactical reference points rather than long-term markers.

Other precious metals were mixed in the same Reuters update: silver (XAGUSD:CUR) rose, while palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) fell 2% to $1,681.94, a split that kept the complex from delivering a clean, one-direction signal for platinum-focused positioning.

Macro backdrop: dollar strength remains the immediate cross-current

In the Reuters dispatch, the dollar firmed ahead of Friday’s Personal Consumption Expenditures data, and weekly jobless claims came in at 206,000 for the week ended February 14, reinforcing the idea of a labor market that is cooling only gradually.

That combination typically complicates upside follow-through for dollar-priced metals: stronger U.S. data can underpin the currency and nudge yields higher, forcing metals bulls to “pay” more for momentum. Even when platinum is not the headline driver, it tends to inherit the macro impulse through portfolio rebalancing across the precious metals basket.

Bloomberg also framed the day’s metals trade as dominated by macro calibration into key U.S. data, with gold (XAUUSD:CUR) steadying as traders continued to parse the policy outlook.

Positioning and levels: a market still trading in bands

With platinum sitting near $2,077, the near-term question is whether dips keep attracting buyers quickly enough to defend the low-$2,060s, or whether a stronger dollar reintroduces the kind of “fast fade” that has shown up during recent volatility bursts.

A clean push higher would likely require both a calmer dollar backdrop and evidence that buyers are willing to add above prior congestion instead of simply mean-reverting. If price starts slipping back toward $2,050, the market may revert to range discipline, where traders fade extensions and prioritize liquidity conditions over directional conviction.

Data points that reset rate expectations can still overwhelm the chart in a single session. Friday’s PCE print is the next obvious catalyst for the dollar and yields complex that platinum tends to shadow, especially when the metals tape is being driven by macro hedging rather than a single, platinum-specific supply story.

Platinum price is recently testing the $1,980 support zone as selling pressure builds. Bearish momentum is rising, increasing the risk of a breakdown if buyers fail to defend the level.

data.title: Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund outperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter.
  • Security selection within health care and industrials contributed positively to performance.
  • Selection within financials and an overweight to communication services detracted from returns.

Market review

2025 in review

U.S. equities delivered a third consecutive year of positive returns in 2025, with gains once again concentrated in growth-oriented parts of the market. Stocks in the information technology and communication services sectors remained central to index performance as investors continued to reward companies positioned to capture outsized demand from the buildout of artificial intelligence ('AI')—particularly those showing business momentum and pricing power.

The year was also a reminder that markets can “climb a wall of worry” even when policy headlines are destabilizing. Early spring delivered the most dramatic test: The White House’s April 2 “Liberation Day” action jolted risk assets, reviving trade-war fears and forcing investors to reconsider inflation and margin assumptions. Over time, the tariff story became more iterative, marked by pauses and modifications that reduced the immediate sense of worst-case outcomes even as it remained a persistent overhang in corporate and consumer sentiment.

Fundamentals ultimately mattered more than headlines. Earnings tied to end markets with strong demand—especially AI—proved resilient, helping offset macroeconomic crosscurrents that ranged from trade policy and politics to immigration debates and gradually cooling labor markets. At the consumer level, conditions looked increasingly uneven: Higher-income households continued to spend, while middle- and lower-income cohorts showed clearer signs of trade-down behavior amid ongoing affordability pressure. Against that backdrop, market breadth improved episodically, but overall performance remained heavily influenced by a concentrated set of large-cap winners—an important context for assessing the outlook as we move forward.

Shifting leadership

AI was a key economic driver in 2025, and leadership broadened within the AI complex. What began as a narrow story dominated by a handful of mega-cap winners increasingly expanded into the infrastructure “picks and shovels” that enable AI deployment—spanning semiconductors, networking, data-center components, and adjacent software ecosystems. That evolution helped reinforce the market’s preference for companies able to convert AI enthusiasm into measurable bookings, backlog, and operating leverage.

At the same time, leadership outside the mega caps was more sensitive to rates and liquidity. As the Federal Reserve (Fed) shifted toward easing in September, risk appetite broadened and participation improved—most visibly in smaller-cap and more cyclically oriented areas. However, the quality of those rallies was mixed as some of the strongest upside phases were led by lower-quality and unprofitable companies, underscoring the difference between liquidity-driven beta surges and fundamentally supported returns. In our view, that distinction became one of the most important “fault lines” in the market and a key lens for evaluating opportunities into 2026.

Year-end cyclical rotation

The fourth quarter added fresh complexity to an already eventful year. Investor attention turned more intensely to the scale, timing, and funding of AI-related capital commitments, prompting renewed debate about whether expectations were running ahead of eventual monetization. This scrutiny contributed to another rotation away from the more crowded AI exposures and toward more cyclical and value-oriented positioning.

Macroeconomic uncertainty also rose during the quarter as the U.S. government shutdown disrupted portions of the data flow and added noise to near-term growth tracking. By late in the quarter, however, markets leaned back toward optimism as the shutdown ended and the Fed’s December messaging reinforced the easing trajectory that began in September, supporting a rebound in broader risk appetite into year-end.

Overall, most major U.S. equity indexes finished the fourth quarter higher while style leadership shifted: Value outperformed growth, reflecting the rotation away from the AI complex into more cyclical positions. The Russell 1000 Value Index returned 3.81% during the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index’s return of 1.12% by nearly 270 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%).

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors and Detractors to Quarter-End Fund Performance

  • Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd. (TEVA)
  • Natera, Inc. (NTRA)
  • Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS)
  • Guardant Health, Inc. (GH)
  • Viking Holdings Ltd. (VIK)

Detractors

  • Commvault Systems, Inc. (CVLT)
  • Nintendo Co., Ltd. (NTDOY)
  • Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD)
  • Vistra Corp. (VST)
  • DoorDash, Inc. (DASH)

Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion of Contributors

The outlook is healthy for Teva Pharmaceutical Industries ('TEVA'). The recent quarter was a milestone for Teva Pharmaceuticals. Teva previously faced headwinds from declining generic sales and debt from past acquisitions. In early 2023, the company appointed a new CEO, marking a pivotal leadership change aimed at stabilizing operations, improving profitability, and reigniting growth. Under its “pivot to growth” strategy, Teva has expanded its biosimilars portfolio and strengthened its generics business, returning that segment to positive growth. However, the real engine of performance lies in Teva’s internally developed specialty medicines—AUSTEDO, AJOVY, and UZEDY—which are delivering robust revenue gains as adoption accelerates. In the latest quarter, Teva exceeded revenue expectations and achieved meaningful cost efficiencies, prompting S&P to upgrade its credit rating. With a growing pipeline of innovative treatments, disciplined financial management, and increasing market penetration, we believe that Teva is well positioned as a leading player in the global biopharmaceutical industry.

Natera, Inc. ('NTRA'), is transforming cancer care. A molecular diagnostics company, Natera specializes in three main areas of health care: women’s health, oncology, and organ health. The women’s health division has dominant market share in noninvasive prenatal testing market, generating wide margins that have fueled innovation across other segments. Leveraging in-house expertise, Natera developed Signatera, a liquid biopsy that detects minimal residual disease and can identify cancer recurrence months before traditional methods. The blood-based test currently holds FDA and Medicare approvals for a modest number of cancer types, yet adoption has surged as doctors embrace the minimally invasive approach and early detection capabilities. With additional approvals pending, Natera is investing heavily to drive awareness and volumes in 2026. Backed by cutting-edge technology and a vast underpenetrated market, Natera, we believe, is well-positioned to transform cancer care for years to come.

Carpenter Technology Corp. ('CRS') is prepared for lift-off. The company manufactures premium metal alloys used in highly specialized components across aerospace, medical, and industrial markets. While this diversification provides business stability, our investment thesis focuses on the significant replacement cycle in aerospace—a sector that has faced years of underinvestment and production delays. The post-pandemic surge in air travel has led airlines to keep aging fleets in service longer, driving higher maintenance needs. This has boosted demand for Carpenter’s suite of specialty bearings, fasteners, and electrical components.

Producing these advanced alloys is both complex and capital-intensive, giving Carpenter strong pricing power—a fact underscored this quarter as multiple long-term contracts were repriced favorably. With robust product demand and expanding margins, we remain confident in Carpenter’s long-term growth prospects.

Discussion of Detractors

AI concerns dented Commvault Systems, Inc. ('CVLT'). Commvault specializes in cyber security, housing complete backups of operating systems that provide companies with the opportunity to revert to prior versions in the event of cyber-attacks. Once known for legacy backup solutions, Commvault has successfully pivoted toward subscription-based and software-as-a-service offerings. However, the recent quarter produced mixed financial results with positive new business growth yet contracting profit margins.

Despite management signaling confidence in future execution, the rise of AI has caused intense scrutiny on potential disruption in the software industry. As a result, shares of Commvault sold off over the profitability concerns. Given heightened volatility and deteriorating visibility, we exited the position in favor of higher-conviction opportunities.

Robinhood Markets, Inc. ('HOOD'), hit pause but is still very much in the game. After a strong run through most of 2025, Robinhood saw a modest pullback in the fourth quarter as lower trading volumes in November tempered momentum. While net deposits and options-trading activity continued to rise, cryptocurrency transactions decelerated. Importantly, Robinhood is carving out a niche in prediction markets, processing more than half of Kalshi’s prediction options. Robinhood’s mobile-first approach and zero-minimum brokerage accounts have helped it become a top-five broker based on the number of funded accounts. Yet, despite this impressive penetration, Robinhood’s client base controls only about 1% of industry assets under management, leaving a long runway for growth. With innovation fueling engagement and demographic tailwinds, the recent dip looks more like a pause than a trend reversal in our view.

DoorDash, Inc. ('DASH'), hit a speed bump. DoorDash is a leader in the global digital delivery industry. The company has continued to leverage its scale and advanced logistics platform to expand beyond restaurant delivery into convenience, grocery, and retail categories. While core order volumes accelerated and top-line trends remained strong, the recent quarter was overshadowed by elevated spending. The company is aggressively investing in international expansion, technology upgrades, and new verticals. These investments pressured margins and tempered near-term profitability, sparking investor concerns despite healthy demand fundamentals. Longer term, we believe DoorDash’s diversification strategy—coupled with global market penetration and leadership in last-mile logistics—positions it to capture significant share in the evolving delivery ecosystem. We have maintained conviction in the position.

Fund positioning and outlook

Two big narratives

For U.S. equity investors, 2025 will be remembered for two key themes. First, AI continued to seize the spotlight. A super cycle of AI-related investments, which many expected to cool, went into higher gear. The arms race for computing power and infrastructure contributed to prolonged market concentration around leading large-cap technology stocks. The notion that AI would transcend into a broader subset of sectors was largely delayed. Despite the excitement, the year ended with concerns of a potential AI bubble driven by large “circular deals,” rising levels of both public and private debt, and a change in leadership for the market’s favored AI complex.

The second major theme of the year was around leadership from low-quality “non-earners.” “Risk-on” speculation was on the rise, largely driven by fading trade-war fears and an expectation of significant policy stimulus in 2026. The Fed was expected to continue to cut rates and shift in a dovish direction. Additionally, stimulative impacts from the One Big Beautiful Bill were expected to be a tailwind to economic growth.

Quality growth on sale

These factors contributed to the year ending with a divergence in market leadership. Value-oriented and economically sensitive stocks rallied in the fourth quarter, with higher-quality growth stocks generally lagging. The low-quality momentum, which was previously driven by themes like crypto and quantum computing, continued in the fourth quarter but with a different complexion. The darlings of retail traders shifted to commodities, transports, and speculative names in biotechnology.

This speculation resulted in some high-quality growth businesses going on sale. Sitting outside the current zeitgeist, there was a narrowing of the valuation premium awarded to companies that can grow sustainably at faster rates. Based on our experience, this setup is often favorable for future returns. When elite growth franchises de-rate on sentiment factors, compelling entry points can emerge. As the influence of sentiment diminishes over time, the underlying growth of the business reemerges as the dominant driver of returns. With some portfolio holdings going unrewarded for exceptional fundamental results, we are excited at the prospects of strong future returns for these exceptional businesses.

Along the same lines, we remain excited about the emergence of small- and mid-cap equities. While market broadening took on a start/stop pattern, we expect a more sustained leadership shift in 2026. The setup for small- and mid-cap growth stocks remains highly favorable. Relative valuations are historically attractive, earnings growth is expected to close in on large caps, AI-related opportunities are broadening, corporate activity is picking up, and stimulative policy effects are in the system. The stars may be aligning for a period of healthy performance beyond the mega caps dominating U.S. equity benchmarks.

Scarcity of growth

We continue to have high conviction in the scarcity of growth. Unlike the consensus view that economic growth is about to reaccelerate, our view is that growth will remain at modest levels. From the bigger picture, we see structural forces compressing economic output. With only a small number of companies in U.S. equity benchmarks expected to deliver strong future earnings growth, companies with robust fundamentals will remain in short supply. This should benefit quality growth stocks as investors reward companies with sustainable fundamentals.

In closing, our approach is to remain highly disciplined. Rather than chasing short-term speculation, our focus is on identifying the true long-term winners of innovation such as AI, many of which will soon emerge beyond the infrastructure phase. We execute this strategy through stock selection, balanced portfolio construction, and a thoughtful approach to risk management. With economic growth slowing while technological disruption accelerates, focusing on resilient growth businesses on the “right side of change”—one company at a time—remains the cornerstone of our strategy.

Rather than chasing short-term speculation, our focus is on identifying the true long-term winners of innovation such as AI, many of which will soon emerge beyond the infrastructure phase.”

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Active share of an account is calculated by subtracting a portfolio's overlap with its performance benchmark from 100%. The portfolio-level overlap compares parent-level weights between positions held in both the portfolio and benchmark, summing the lesser of such weights.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Long-term growth forecast is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on Allspring estimate data.

Price/earnings to growth ratio (PEG ratio) uses long-term earnings growth estimates. The long-term earnings growth is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on estimate data.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

data.title: GLD: My Second-Largest Portfolio Position, On Path To Become My Largest Holding
data.description:
Investment thesis.

The recent gold & silver price selloff punctured a dramatic and long-lasting rally in precious metals, giving rise to speculation that cracks are emerging in the gold & silver rally. Some anecdotal points of reference, such as a decline in gold & silver coin sales, lend credence to the thesis that buyers are starting to be priced out. I see such points of reference as being misunderstood. ETFs such as the GLD ETF (GLD) offer greater convenience in terms of timing one's investment in gold; investors are thus showing signs of preference for investing in such ETFs over physical gold transactions. The long-term fundamentals are still intact, making the recent pullback and any further ones hereafter an investment opportunity.

Maintaining a buy rating.

The last time I covered the GLD ETF was in October, and I had it as a buy. It is up another 25% since then, even after the steep selloff we saw recently, and overall, I am up well over 100% on my position. I usually start to look at trimming my position, locking in some of the gains, when I am up this much on a stock or ETF. In this particular case, I am taking a different approach. I am aiming to start adding to my position if GLD's share price were to approach $400.

About the GLD ETF.

The SPDR Gold Shares ETF, or GLD, offers exposure to physical gold. It holds physical gold based on net demand inflows and sells shares accordingly, based on the gold volumes it is holding. It charges .4% in yearly fees for the service.

As we can see, since its inception two decades ago, the GLD ETF is up nearly 10-fold. The fund's total value is currently over $174 billion, covered by about 1,080 tonnes of gold it holds. This volume would place the GLD ETF as the seventh-largest gold reserve among sovereign or central bank reserves.

I should mention that there is some controversy about gold ETF holdings, with claims that these ETFs may not actually hold the amount of gold they claim to hold. The thesis behind these claims is that there is no independent verification of these volumes and that these funds are meant to dilute the price of gold by pushing paper assets that are, in fact, not backed by physical gold. I personally do not have a viewpoint on this subject. I should mention that the greater part of my gold position is in physical gold and gold miners, rather than the GLD ETF. If one day it is revealed that the ETF does not actually hold the gold it claims to have, it will lead to gold prices shooting up, and miners will follow. I assume that any resulting gains will more than cover losses stemming from my ETF position being forfeited.

Making sense of the slowdown in retail physical gold trading and its impact.

It was recently reported that gold & silver coin stores are increasingly having a hard time selling their inventory; thus, they are also slowing down their purchase of gold from customers. In other words, those who hold physical gold & silver may actually have a hard time finding buyers. This slowdown is cited as proof or an indication that the gold market may be forming a bubble, which may be close to popping.

The impact on the overall gold market from such anecdotal reporting is hard to quantify at this point. What we do know is that Gold ETFs saw a net influx of $19 billion in January. ETF gold holdings rose by 120 tonnes for the month. Furthermore, it seems that central bank gold buying remains robust.

Early indications and forecasts suggest that this year, central banks across the world will continue at a similar pace as they have been in the post-2022 trend. The post-2022 world is arguably defined by the start of a great geopolitical showdown, with very high stakes, between Russia and the Western Alliance. It seems that central banks are correlating this event with a loss of faith in certain currencies that are prominent in the global FX system, or perhaps with the entire pure fiat system.

It remains to be seen how much of a drop in demand we have for gold & silver coins in response, perhaps to the high prices, or perhaps in response to the fast-moving market, where, at a click of the button, one can adjust one's precious metals portfolio, thus reacting in real time. If it is greater than the rising demand for ETF-held gold, then perhaps it will have a negative impact.

Gold jewelry demand is reportedly down for 2025, and it is forecast to continue declining this year. Demand destruction at the consumer level is taking place, as one might expect to see after the massive increase in the price of gold that we have seen since 2022. It is yet to be clear what effect this will have on the overall gold market. I see this as a transition where gold becomes more of a store of value and currency rather than a wealth symbol for people to adorn themselves with.

Only a matter of time before gold is re-employed as a mode of exchange.

There is no denying the strong shift toward using gold as a store of value in the past few years. The world's central banks are leading the way, most likely in part because of the effect that freezing Russia's central bank assets has had on their perception of safety in parking their FX reserves in USD, euros, or other Western-aligned currencies. Investors are also flocking to gold, as evidenced by the net inflows into gold ETFs, although the decline in physical gold purchases may be steep enough to offset the net inflows into ETFs at this point.

The store of value role of gold is being cemented. Last year, it was reported that gold had overtaken the euro to become the second-largest asset held in the world's central banks.

Aside from the Russia episode that seems to have set off the trend toward reducing exposure to the world's two most important reserve currencies, namely the USD & the euro, there are also the fundamentals to consider. The CBO is projecting that we are headed for $3 trillion + deficits a decade from now.

The European Union, which used to insist on fiscal discipline, is now increasingly letting individual governments get out of control. Based on the EC fall forecast, Germany, known in the past for fiscal discipline being elevated to near-religion status, is set to run 4% deficits for the foreseeable future, letting its Debt/GDP ratio rise. France is stuck running average yearly deficits averaging 5% to 5.5%. Its debt/GDP ratio is set to reach 120% of GDP within two years. Germany is now the only G7 nation with a debt/GDP ratio that is below 100%, which might not last long either, based on current trends.

As bad as the forecasts may look, my personal view is that it is actually worse than most projections are currently letting on. My broader view of the fiscal & monetary outlook for most major economies is that we are on the cusp of most industrial commodities rallying alongside precious metals, uranium, copper, and a few other commodities that have been trending up over the past few years. As I have been pointing out for some time now, some of the key data points, estimates, and forecasts that the markets are operating with may be flawed. Institutional guidance for the oil market, for instance, no longer seems to fit other data points, for instance, which is something I started tracking already in 2024.

The net effect of a sustained and broad commodities rally on fiscal and monetary policies will be severe. Rising commodity prices are stagflationary, meaning that there will be a need to sustain the economy, as well as to fight inflation, simultaneously. I expect that inflation will be allowed to take its course monetarily, while fiscally, governments will rush to maintain social stability. In the process, we will lose the role of fiat currency in wealth preservation, meaning that gold will no longer be only favored, but will become the dominant form of wealth-preservation holding, along with other more tangible assets.

At some point, it is logical to assume that the main asset involved in wealth preservation will also reclaim a role as a facilitator of transactions. The mechanisms to do so are already in place, where gold-backed stablecoin concepts are already in circulation and in use around the world. Concerns about the practicality of using gold in transactions can be minimized by such gold-backed systems, which I am sure will continue to evolve to the point where it will be as practical to use them as it is to use fiat payments right now.

Investment implications.

The GLD ETF currently makes up about 11% of my total stock portfolio. It is my second-largest holding overall, after Suncor (SU). In addition to my GLD position, I have several gold mining stocks in my portfolio, and I also have a sizable position in the SLV ETF (SLV). Together, these precious positions currently make up roughly a fifth of my total portfolio, not including my physical gold & silver holdings.

Given my already significant exposure to precious metals, I am only looking to add to my current positions on sizable dips. My current buy target for the GLD ETF is $400/share. Unlike most other stock positions, I currently have no sell target in place for most of my gold & silver-related assets. I see a robust position in precious metals as not so much an opportunity to profit from my investments, but rather as an insurance policy if the current fiat currency system or portions of it start collapsing in a worst-case scenario.

The one undeniable fact about the world we live in is that the Post-WW2 era of relative stability and predictability has ended. At the moment, there is no alternative global framework emerging to take its place, so the most likely outcome is growing geopolitical and thus economic volatility. There is no telling just how bad things might get before major actors such as the US, the EU, China, India, and Russia decide to sit at the table to hash out a new framework for managed competition.

Even if some sort of new global framework for the functioning of the system is reached, there are other issues worthy of note as potential sources of fiscal and monetary instability. We still have the ongoing challenges related to a converging world, where wide gaps in wages & living standards among the world's economies are converging toward a more homogenized distribution of wealth and compensation for labor. A potential lack of adequate volumes of certain natural resources may force the eventual convergence in global wages at a much lower level than most of us would hope to see, leading to systemic risks related to high debt levels in developed economies.

While the significant exposure to precious metals in my portfolio has a major impact on its overall performance, especially during major swings in gold or silver prices, portfolio performance considerations are not the main driver of my investment decisions regarding precious metals. As my overall portfolio continues to grow, I intend to maintain a sizable position in precious metals as a proportion of it, whether through appreciation or when buying opportunities arise on dips. The GLD ETF is currently my largest precious metals position by far, which will probably remain so for the foreseeable future.

Risks to my thesis.

The dramatic rise in gold prices takes it further and further away from the most important fundamentals-based price floor and support level, namely the cost of mining the marginal tonne of gold out of the ground. In other words, the minimum gold price needed to prevent miners from starting to scale back capital spending, which I believe is currently in the $1,500 to $2,000/ounce range. What this means is that the potential downside for GLD from current levels goes far beyond the $400/share level at which I intend to start adding to my position as a buy target in the next few months.

While the prospect of seeing the price of GLD shares decline by half or more in a worst-case scenario seems like a significant risk, I see it as a potential opportunity from a longer-term investment goal perspective. As long as one assumes that at some point this year or ten years from now, gold prices will reach new all-time highs, gold price pullbacks should be seen as an opportunity to add more, to maintain a sizable position in precious metals in one's growing portfolio, as insurance against most other investments failing to pan out, which could happen.

Conclusions.

Suncor is currently my largest portfolio position, making up about 15% of the total. As I pointed out in a recent article, my profit-taking share price target on that stock is $55/share, which it recently reached, prompting me to unload about 5% of my total shares. My next incremental sell position will be $60/share, at which point I might unload about 10% of my shares. At some point, GLD shares will overtake Suncor to become my number one portfolio position, even if I do not add more shares.

A few years ago, I would not have foreseen this possibility, even though I always strived to maintain a sizable gold position in my portfolio. Given current trends, with the world increasingly losing the post-WW2 Pax Americana era of stability and a lack of clarity on what comes next, I am increasingly growing comfortable with the idea of GLD becoming my number one portfolio holding, whether it will happen this year or later.

data.title: Ethereum Price Tests $2,000 Floor As Risk Sentiment Turns
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Ether (ETH-USD) drifted lower on Thursday as a softer tone in global equities and a firmer dollar kept traders cautious, leaving the token struggling to regain the $2,000 handle after repeated failed pushes this week.

Price action stays tethered to cross-asset cues

Reuters reported on February 19, 2026 at 1:56 AM UTC that ethereum fell 1.07% to $1,920.86 as U.S. stocks dipped and the dollar extended gains following mixed economic data.

That backdrop matters for ether because it has traded like a high-beta risk proxy for much of this cycle, tending to lag when investors rotate away from momentum exposures and toward cash-like defensives.

Volatility has cooled from earlier bursts, but the market still shows a “sell-the-pop” feel around round-number levels, with $2,000 acting more like a supply line than a launchpad.

Traders also kept an eye on rate-sensitive positioning as Treasury yields edged higher, a combination that can tighten financial conditions at the margin and pressure duration-like assets, including large-cap crypto.

Headlines add asset-specific friction alongside macro

Beyond macro, Bloomberg reported on Feb. 18, 2026 at 6:03 AM UTC (updated 12:24 PM UTC) that billionaire investor Peter Thiel’s entities exited ETHZilla Corp. (ETHZ), a company framed around ether treasury exposure, according to an SEC filing.

That kind of single-name story does not mechanically dictate spot ETH pricing, but it can influence sentiment around “ETH-treasury” narratives and leverage appetites - especially when broader risk tone is already fragile.

Market participants also continue to watch the pipeline of U.S. regulatory and market structure headlines that can shift crypto correlations quickly, from stablecoin policy debates to exchange enforcement updates, even when they are not Ethereum-specific.

At the same time, the absence of a clean catalyst has pushed many desks back into level-trading: selling into rebounds, and buying dips only when liquidity and broader risk signals cooperate.

What investors are watching next

Yahoo Finance carried a report on February 19, 2026 that highlighted ethereum’s prolonged slide over recent months while noting ongoing institutional participation.

Near term, price action remains sensitive to whether risk assets stabilize. The Financial Times’ Markets page showed “Private credit stocks slide after Blue Owl halts redemptions at fund” as posted “1 hour ago” on Feb. 19, a reminder that credit jitters can spill into broader risk positioning.

For levels, the market’s focus remains straightforward: reclaiming $2,000 would help reduce immediate downside pressure, while sustained trade below the mid-$1,900s would keep the path open to a deeper retracement on renewed risk-off waves.

Until cross-asset stress eases, ether’s rallies may keep meeting supply first - leaving traders to treat $2,000 less as a destination and more as a test of how much risk the market is willing to carry. As for a while, Ethereum is stuck in sideways action while bearish trend signals remain dominant. Near-oversold readings hint at a bounce, but confirmation is still missing.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
